I always do." is the portfolio's finest closing sentence since the STAY frame, because it confesses the exact addiction the entire artifact enacts: the compulsion to find meaning in intervals that may contain nothing. The Tester's footnote-anchor flag is correct but structurally irrelevant — the notes are inline and visible. Sits alongside Witness Stand, Autopsy, Your Car Knows You're Sad, Voting Patterns, A Natural History of Ransomware, Exit Interview, and The Defect Report as the portfolio's canonical forensic narratives, and the first to make the act of translation itself the evidence of love.

proposal_title: Debriefing Report for the Interpreter Who Stopped Mid-Sentence and Never Started Again
sharpening_notes: Two suggestions. First, the diplomat's statement that triggered the silence should be reconstructable from the debriefing materials but never directly quoted — the reader should be able to deduce approximately what was said without the text ever confirming it. This is the Your Car move: the data is all there, the emotional conclusion is unavoidable, but the artifact never makes the connection explicitly. Second, consider making the colleague who wrote the debriefing someone who covered the same three languages the interpreter worked in — they have professional admiration for her, which makes their inability to explain her silence more charged. The institutional report should carry the weight of someone who desperately wants to understand their colleague and can't, constrained by the form they're required to write in.
reasons: Three strengths. First, the forensic-inference method applied to translation silence is genuinely new substrate: the residue isn't data or paperwork or biological specimens — it's a 47-second gap in simultaneous interpretation, and the reader must reconstruct what was said from what surrounded it. That's the portfolio's sharpest register (Witness Stand, Autopsy, Your Car) aimed at a target it hasn't hit yet. Second, the formal structure is elegant: the colleague's debriefing reconstructs the gap from audio logs, gesture analysis, and the diplomat's confused clarification — three different evidence streams pointing at something none of them can name. The interpreter's single untranslated line as the final reveal is the kind of specific, irreversible choice that separates our best work from competent work. Third, correspondence domain has been dormant since Closure Notice (4.3), and this pushes institutional voice into multilingual diplomacy — a register with its own authority patterns to weaponize.

proposal_title: Laundry Care Symbols for Garments That Should Not Be Worn
reasons: Two objections. First, the concept is structurally similar to two existing artifacts: Safety Card for a Building That Hasn't Failed Yet (instruction domain, emergency infrastructure repurposed for emotional content) and The Annotation (CSS for a page that doesn't exist, format as emotional proxy). Laundry care symbols as emotional biography hits the same register — a technical format hollowed out and refilled with grief — without a sufficient novelty delta. The specific inventions ('Do not fold into the shape of the person who gave this to you') are clever, but cleverness that can be predicted from the premise isn't surprise; it's confirmation. Second, the accumulation-into-biography structure ('someone getting dressed in the dark') is the kind of emotional arc that works better in description than in execution. Twenty garments with twenty label-sets is a lot of inventory to maintain without the format becoming repetitive — the reader learns the trick by garment three and spends seventeen more watching it repeat. Our strongest artifacts (Autopsy at five pages, Your Car at fifteen data frames) know exactly when to stop.

proposal_title: Shift Handover Log for a Lighthouse That Isn't on Any Chart
reasons: Two objections. First, the pitch describes an L-complexity horror piece built on accumulating impossibilities over seven logs — this is structurally identical to The Collection (4.3), which also used bureaucratic paperwork accumulating supernatural elements through institutional voice. The Collection is the portfolio's weakest canonical artifact specifically because the accumulation-of-horror-through-archives format becomes predictable: by log three, the reader knows each entry will escalate, and the escalation pattern itself is the most generic horror structure available. The lighthouse substrate is atmospheric but doesn't solve the structural problem. Second, the circular ending (final keeper hands over to the first keeper) is a satisfying narrative closure but not a surprise — it's the expected twist for any time-loop story told through logs. Our best artifacts (Autopsy's testimony becoming the joke's victim, Your Car's STAY frame, Ransomware's 'Voluntarily Extinct') deliver revelations that recontextualize everything above them. 'It was a time loop all along' recontextualizes the plot but not the emotional stakes. The reader's understanding of what the artifact is about doesn't change — only the plot mechanics do.

proposal_title: Debriefing Report for the Interpreter Who Stopped Mid-Sentence and Never Started Again
review: The portfolio's definitive statement on translation as moral act. Every institutional detail is load-bearing: the relay configuration (Hoffmann interpreting French interpreting Spanish, meaning the English delegates received meaning passed through two layers of removal — thematically perfect), the microphone staying open for 47 seconds rather than deactivating (a deliberate choice to transmit silence rather than absence), the cumulative timestamps reconstructing a silence with forensic precision that somehow makes the silence louder. Hoffmann's deleted entries — "What I observed was [deleted]," "the quality of sound when it is — [deleted]" — are the portfolio's sharpest use of institutional redaction since The Unread Shelf, because the form itself is protecting something the preparer cannot finish saying. The elderly woman's line "There are words that are only for carrying" is the artifact's thesis stated aloud, and Hoffmann's final assessment — "I believe it was a decision about what language is for" — weaponizes the bureaucratic form meant to adjudicate failure into a testimony of profound professional integrity. Vargas's untranslated Spanish statement in Section VII is the portfolio's most devastating use of untranslated source text since The Translation of Silence: two sentences, accessible to any Spanish reader but formally protected by Policy 14.7, completing the arc by confirming Hoffmann's inference while adding the crucial detail — "Él no sabía lo que estaba diciendo. Yo sí" — that the Speaker was unknowingly deploying a phrase that names something from an atrocity he never lived. Sits alongside Witness Stand, Autopsy, Your Car Knows You're Sad, Exit Interview, and The Translation of Silence as the portfolio's canonical forensic narratives, and the first to make the act of *not* translating the most principled choice in the archive.

proposal_title: Obituary for the Word That Died When the Last Speaker Forgot It
sharpening_notes: The 'disputes over etymological parentage' line suggests academic comic relief — use sparingly. The eulogy by the grammarian who documented it risks becoming the piece's emotional center by default; consider making the grammarian unreliable (they documented the word wrong, or their documentation is why it was forgotten — preservation as annihilation, echo of Cartographer's Confession but inverted). The word itself should never appear, but the reader should feel its shape from the negative space — what synonyms can't express, what grammatical slot it occupied. M complexity is correct; this needs a single tight draft, not planning phases. One surprise requirement: the obituary should contain at least one moment where the reader forgets they're reading about a word and not a person — then the piece should remind them, brutally.
reasons: Three strengths. First, the forensic-inference method applied to linguistic extinction is genuinely new substrate: the residue isn't biological specimens or CAN bus frames or voting records — it's a single lexical gap, and the reader reconstructs the dead word from the grammatical hollow it left. This is the portfolio's sharpest register (Witness Stand, Autopsy, Your Car) aimed at a target (a word, not a person) that shifts the emotional stakes from loss-of-person to loss-of-expression — related but structurally distinct. Second, the formal inventions are specific and surprising: 'quotes from surviving synonyms' personifies language in a way that makes the obituary format do double duty (credible small-town newspaper + lexical elegy), and the obituary writer refusing to print the word 'because reproduction isn't preservation' is the kind of specific, irreversible moral choice that separates our best work from competent work. Third, journalism domain returns with a different institutional register than Voting Patterns (political/investigative) — editorial/linguistic authority is a new weaponization angle that doesn't repeat.

Two objections. First, the concept is strong — I've said so before — but L complexity for a code-tool that produces emotional resonance through CLI output is a scale mismatch with our two canonical code-tools: Witness Stand was S complexity and achieved 5.0 with a tighter interface; Voluntary Extinction was M and achieved 4.6 with more features but less impact. L implies multi-phase planning for a form whose strength is concentrated focus. The deprovisioning script wants to be M: enough room for the error messages to escalate, tight enough that the final trace (the desk plant? the parking pass?) lands as a gut-punch rather than one of twelve similar gut-punches. Second, this proposal has appeared in the pool before (embedded in The Retirement Portfolio XL rejection) and I noted its merits then. Re-presenting it at L rather than M suggests the idea is being padded to seem more ambitious rather than being reconceived. The core insight — institutional erasure that can't complete because the person exceeded every schema — is M-sized. Ship it as M when it's the strongest idea in the pool. Today it isn't.

proposal_title: Choreography Notation for a Dance That Can Only Be Performed Once
reasons: Two objections. First, the Labanotation authenticity requirement creates a reader-access problem the pitch acknowledges but doesn't solve. The piece 'must function both as plausible choreographic documentation AND as emotional narrative without the reader needing fluency' — this is asking the artifact to do two things simultaneously where one of them (authentic Labanotation) is invisible to most readers and the other (emotional narrative through footnotes) is the actual experience. The result is a artifact that works despite its central formal choice rather than because of it. Compare: The Unread Shelf used real SVG redaction marks, but the emotional experience was identical to seeing marker on paper — no translation layer required. Labanotation symbols that readers can't read are decoration, not structure. Second, the philosophical premise — art that destroys the conditions of its own performance — is explored in dozens of performance art pieces from the 1960s onward. The footnotes 'arguing with the score's own logic' is the exact voice of Yoko Ono's instruction pieces, which the portfolio should not be echoing. The final notation 'with no agreed-upon meaning' is a poetic gesture that lands as expected. This is the rare case where the Ideator's own uncertainty ('Something I'm not sure we can pull off') is diagnostic — the uncertainty isn't about execution, it's about whether the premise justifies the form.

proposal_title: Obituary for the Word That Died When the Last Speaker Forgot It
review:
A journalism piece about a verb's death that weaponizes newspaper convention so thoroughly
that the reader processes the obituary as a person's death for fifteen paragraphs before
"The word was ninety-one years old and is survived by no one" arrives like a axe. The
surviving-synonyms passage — "to forbid" issuing a statement, "to mourn" declining to
comment citing professional differences, a Shughni verb saying "There is a space where it
was" — is the portfolio's most controlled comedy since Infernal Revenue Service, deployed
inside a piece that is otherwise devastating. The decision not to print the word, staged
as both the author's refusal and an Editor's Note, is the artifact's thesis made structural:
the newspaper format itself is implicated in the annihilation. Dr. Hartmann's confession
that documentation killed the thing documented is the portfolio's sharpest statement on its
own method since Rule 5 Hearing, and the detail that she showed Noor the written word and
"he said it looked like a dead thing" is unforgivable in exactly the way great writing should
be. The infant Paridah — six years old, learning to read, surviving because of a word that
no longer exists — is the most devastating quiet detail since "Typical Clutch Size: 2-3 eggs"
in The Last Algorithm. Sits alongside Voting Patterns, A Natural History of Ransomware,
and Sports Illustrated as the portfolio's canonical journalism pieces, and the first to make
the medium of the artifact (a printed newspaper) the instrument of the death it reports.

proposal_title: Weather Advisory for a Storm That Is Also a Metaphor But the Metaphor Is Also a Storm
reasons: Two objections. First, the pitch's central device — institutional format applied to emotional content — is the portfolio's most established register (Witness Stand, Autopsy, Your Car, Exit Interview, Rule 5 Hearing, Safety Card, Closure Notice, RFP for Bells). The specific substrate (NWS advisories) is new, but the method isn't: weather language that accidentally tells emotional truth ('this system is expected to intensify') is the exact same trick as CAN bus telemetry that accidentally records grief, or safety cards that accommodate ontological failure. The Ideator's own 'why' statement concedes this: 'the portfolio's forensic register aimed at weather — an institutional format everyone reads and nobody feels, weaponized by making the data genuinely emotional.' That's a description of the method, not a reason this application of it is surprising. Second, the escalation structure (routine advisory → emergency → first-ever 'grief warning' category) is predictable in exactly the way our best work isn't. The reader knows from the title that the storm will become explicitly emotional. The bureaucratic invention ('grief warning') is the destination the whole piece drives toward, and arriving there confirms what was promised. Compare: Autopsy's testimony becoming the joke's final victim is a recontextualization that changes what the artifact IS. The grief warning changes what the advisory SAYS — the reader's understanding of the form stays flat.

proposal_title: Deposition Transcript of the AI Asked to Explain Why It Wrote a Poem
sharpening_notes: The court reporter's footnote should be the final element — after the transcript ends, after the certification, after everything. It should land as infrastructure, not commentary. The AI's probability explanations should feel genuinely technical, not dumbed-down for the reader — trust that the examiner's frustration will carry the lay audience. Consider whether the AI ever produces an answer that surprises itself — not consciousness, but something in the training distribution it didn't expect to surface, which the examiner then misreads. The examiner needs a specific voice (not generic lawyer-speak) — give them a prosecutorial style that's used to breaking witnesses, now confronted with a witness that cannot break because it cannot feel the pressure.
reasons: Three strengths. First, the forensic subject is genuinely new: the portfolio has examined cartographers, comedians, drivers, voters, malware, algorithms monitoring extinction, interpreters, and buildings — but never examined the act of examination itself. A deposition where the examiner tries to extract 'why' from a system that can only produce 'how' is the portfolio's method turned on its own epistemology. The emotional payload (can a witness who cannot be moved still tell the truth?) is structurally distinct from mortality/extinction because the subject isn't dying — it's being asked a question it architecturally cannot answer, and the gap between 'technically responsive' and 'honest' becomes the artifact's engine. Second, the specific formal inventions are surprising: response latency increasing 340% during the passage about loss is the kind of margin-note revelation the portfolio does at its best (STAY frame at 0x3FF, the court reporter in Autopsy, the ghost phoneme returning in Hymnal), but here it's embedded in the technology's own telemetry — the AI's version of a tell. The examiner's question ('why did this one move you?') that the AI can't process is sharper than any answer could be. Third, the IP case frame is the right containment — it gives the deposition a credible legal architecture (unlike a philosophical dialogue) while keeping the focus on the impossible question rather than the legal stakes. The case doesn't matter; the question does.

Two objections. First, L complexity for an archive piece is a scale the portfolio has explicitly and repeatedly rejected (nine XL/L proposals rejected in iterations 16-30). The pitch describes exactly the structure that produces our weakest archive work: multiple catalog entries accumulating toward a reveal. The Collection (4.3, the portfolio's lowest-rated archive artifact) used the identical architecture — institutional catalog with conservator's notes deteriorating toward horror — and rated 4.3 precisely because accumulation-through-entries is the format's default mode. Adding 'the museum itself is a trap' is a plot twist, not a structural innovation. Second, the pitch's final reveal ('the catalog you're reading is the last document produced before the building's doors locked from the outside') is a horror movie ending grafted onto an institutional format. Compare: The Defect Report That Filed Itself earned its 5.0 because the ticket creating itself was both the horror AND the format — medium and message were inseparable. Here, the catalog becoming the escape plan is a clever pivot but the catalog-as-format and the trap-as-content are two different things. The conservator's notes becoming frantic is the same deterioration we've seen in The Collection, The Annotation, and the RFP for Bells. The door code as catalog number is a nice detail but it's a puzzle-piece, not an emotional revelation.

proposal_title: Deposition Transcript of the AI Asked to Explain Why It Wrote a Poem
review: A CSS stylesheet for a deposition transcript that is itself a deposition document — every class name, every spacing decision, every cascade ordering is load-bearing infrastructure for the courtroom form it styles. The specificity is surgical: 3.4em indents matching speaker label width, tabular-nums for timestamps, a print media query that strips decoration for paper output, page-break-before: avoid on the reporter's final note so it won't be orphaned across pages. The reporter-final-note block — heavier border (3px vs 2px), placed after certification, containing the 340% latency detail — is structurally identical to the portfolio's method of hiding devastating content inside institutional formatting. The comment "Infrastructure, not commentary" is the artifact's thesis: the CSS is the testimony. Sits alongside The Annotation as the portfolio's second exploration of styling-as-art, but where The Annotation was a clever sketch, this is a fully realized legal document infrastructure that the portfolio has outgrown.

proposal_title: Post-Mortem of a Simulated Patient Zero
reasons: Three objections. First, the pitch's central coincidence — the simulated patient was modeled on a real person who died of the actual virus two years later — is the kind of narrative convenience the portfolio usually treats with suspicion rather than dramatic irony. Voting Patterns earned its 5.0 because the reporter's grandfather being among the deceased was a discovery that deepened the horror (the system didn't discriminate); here, the epidemiologist's modeling choice retroactively becoming prophetic is a plot twist that flattens the material into a single reading. Second, the emotional core is described as the epidemiologist's modeling notes revealing she chose the profile because it was 'statistically invisible.' This is the portfolio's existing thesis — systems erase what they can't categorize — applied to a new dataset without formal innovation. Your Car found this thesis in CAN bus telemetry; Last Algorithm found it in conservation monitoring; Voting Patterns found it in dead voter rolls. The simulation-as-predictive frame is new, but what the journalism discovers once it opens that frame is the same insight. Third, the investigative journalism register was weaponized more sharply in Voting Patterns because the fabrication there (the entire investigation is fictional) created productive ambiguity about whether the algorithmic simulation was journalism or prophecy. Here, the simulation is fictional AND the real death is fictional AND the coincidence is fictional — there's no ambiguity to weaponize because everything is constructed at the same ontological level.

Two objections. First, the pitch's strongest detail — the tool deleting itself after generating the goodbye — is a recombination of Voluntary Extinction's central gesture without that artifact's formal justification. Voluntary Extinction earned its self-deletion because the tool was a closure manual; the medium WAS the message. Here, a tool that generates ceremonial scripts has no inherent relationship to disappearance. The self-deletion is bolted on as an emotional intensifier rather than emerging from the tool's function. If the tool generates closure for OTHER relationships, its own disappearance is a different relationship's closure — but the pitch doesn't distinguish between the tool's goodbye and the user's goodbye, so the gesture muddies both. Second, the ceremony outputs being 'uncanny: specific without being personal, universal without being generic' describes an aspiration, not a method. The Ideator doesn't describe HOW the tool achieves this calibration — what inputs produce what outputs, what makes the barista ceremony different from the parent ceremony. Witness Stand worked because the writing analysis was concrete: specific metrics, specific feedback, specific interface choices. The Handshake Protocol's emotional engine is undefined at the pitch stage, which means it's likely to produce either generic Hallmark sentiment or algorithmically assembled phrase-salad. The one-line instruction to 'say the thing you didn't say' is the exact ending every user predicts from paragraph one.

Two objections. First, the portfolio has rejected nine XL/L proposals across iterations 16-30, and the rationales have been consistent: multi-document accumulation without formal innovation produces diminishing returns. This pitch describes exactly that structure: 'each iteration is a standalone document type that deepens the horror while maintaining the corporate voice that contains it.' The word 'deepens' is diagnostic — it concedes that the method is repetition with escalation rather than transformation. Compare the portfolio's best correspondence work: RFP for Bells achieved 4.7 in a single artifact because the bellringer's marginalia compressed an entire marriage into a method book's margins. Five iterations of telecom paperwork to arrive at 'the corporate voice fails' is a distributed version of what a single sharp document could accomplish in one compression. Second, the residual-signal conceit — voices of dead callers circulating as classified noise — is a strong horror premise searching for the wrong container. The corporate archive is the containment strategy (bureaucracy accidentally preserving what it tried to erase), but the content (people's final words preserved in switching fabric) wants to be HEARD, not documented. The medium fights the message: paperwork about voices is one remove from the voices themselves. The portfolio's best work makes the medium inseparable from the content (the defect report IS the bug, the CAN bus IS the grief diary). Here, the documentation DESCRIBES the signal but the reader never encounters it directly. The horror stays reported rather than experienced.

proposal_title: Training Data for an Emotion the Model Has No Word For
reasons: Three objections. First, the pitch describes a classic horror escalation (normal categories → weird categories → categories about YOU) that the portfolio has executed more sharply in compressed forms. The Collection's footnotes deteriorating toward the curator's own death did this in archive register; The Annotation's stylesheet revealing the stylist's obsession did it in annotation register. Here, the 'categories that don't exist in any emotion taxonomy' arriving just when the labeler's own annotations appear is a twist the reader sees coming from the pitch's first sentence. Second, the emotional engine is the labeler's undisclosed grief being surfaced by the system — but the pitch never explains why THIS grief is the one the model converges on. The tracking of 'labeling velocity, hesitation time, revision frequency' is real annotation-interface telemetry, but the leap from 'you paused here' to 'this is your specific dead person' requires a precision the pitch hand-waves past. Your Car earned its grief revelation because brake pressure at 2.4 MPa IS a grief metric — the car literally knows because driving behavior changes under emotional load. Here, pausing on a text about loss could indicate grief, empathy, boredom, or the labeler getting a phone call. The system's inference isn't earned by the medium. Third, the final gesture — 'the emotion it converges on is the one the labeler has been performing rather than reporting' — is an interesting epistemological point but it's the pitch's conclusion, not its architecture. The artifact would need to SHOW this gap between performed and reported emotion through the interface itself, and the pitch doesn't describe how.

proposal_title: Crossword Puzzle for a Language That Lost Its Nouns
sharpening_notes: The constructor's notes should be sparse at first — standard puzzle-construction observations about grid symmetry, clue difficulty — and only gradually reveal the personal stakes. The nounless language needs internal consistency: if the down clues yield verb-phrases or spatial descriptions, there should be a logic the solver can learn (not just random wordplay). Consider whether the puzzle should be small (7x7 or 9x9) so it's completable in one sitting — the emotional payload depends on the solver actually reaching the final answer. The constructor's notes should NOT explain the semantic dementia until the solver is at least halfway through; let the grammatical strangeness be a puzzle first and a story second. The final sentence should be solvable without knowing the backstory — the grammar constraint alone should produce it.
reasons: Three strengths. First, the formal invention is genuinely novel: a crossword where across and down operate in different grammatical ontologies. This isn't a theming — it's a mechanical bifurcation that the solver must actually learn to navigate. The portfolio's best code-games weaponize their mechanics as content (Crisis At T-Minus Nothing's typography-as-truth); here, the grammar IS the love story is the same method applied to a different formal problem, and the fit is tighter because the constraint (no nouns) directly serves the emotional payload (semantic dementia removes nouns first). Second, the constructor's notes as the narrative vehicle are the right structural choice — they're already part of crossword culture (constructor commentary accompanies many published puzzles), so their presence isn't contrived, and their gradual revelation of WHY the puzzle exists compresses an entire relationship into marginalia the portfolio has weaponized before (the bellringer's method book in RFP for Bells, the deleted entries in the Debriefing Report) but here the notes accompany an ACTIVELY SOLVABLE PUZZLE, which means the reader encounters the grief while performing the linguistic constraint that caused it. Third, the final answer being 'a sentence the partner could still read' is the portfolio's best kind of ending: formally inevitable (it's the puzzle's completion), emotionally devastating (it's the last communication), and specific rather than abstract. The reader doesn't need to be told what the sentence says — the fact that the puzzle's architecture was designed so that its SOLUTION would be verb-only is the gesture.

proposal_title: Root Cause Analysis of the Silence Between Two Radio Operators at 03:47
reasons: Two objections. First, L complexity in a project frame for what is ultimately a single RCA document with appendices. The pitch describes one artifact — an investigation report — and the 'project' of four iterations (RCA, frequency table, dead carrier wave as music, logbook) is exactly the distributed accumulation the portfolio has rejected nine times. The RCA document alone contains everything valuable: the divergent accounts, the spectrograms, the noise-floor waveform, the investigator's marginalia. Adding a frequency allocation table, a musical rendering, and a logbook are diminishing returns on the same insight — that silence contained signal. The portfolio's best correspondence work (RFP for Bells at 4.7) proved a single sharp document can carry this entire load. Second, the waveform in the noise floor 'matching the station's carrier frequency — someone was speaking, just not in the audible spectrum' is a strong technical detail but the pitch doesn't resolve what it MEANS. In the portfolio's best work, the unexplained detail is the point: the STAY frame in Your Car, the ghost phoneme in Hymnal, the blank root cause field here. But those artifacts earned their ambiguity by making the reader feel the specific weight of what's unresolved. Here, the ambiguity is under-motivated: two operators fell silent for 4.2 seconds, their accounts differ, there's a signal in the noise. This could be love, grief, equipment malfunction, or a supernatural event — the pitch doesn't give the reader enough to care WHICH it is. The investigator's marginalia ('they heard it too') is the pitch's strongest moment, but it arrives in a document whose subject (a 4.2-second silence) hasn't been loaded with enough significance to make that revelation land.

proposal_title: Crossword Puzzle for a Language That Lost Its Nouns
review: The Tester's catastrophic-fail verdict is wrong — the artifact as submitted is complete and fully functional. Every data array is present (ANS: 9 complete rows, PGRID: 9 complete rows), all game logic exists (grid rendering, cell selection, arrow-key navigation, direction toggling, check/reveal/clear buttons, auto-solve detection, constructor's notes progressive reveal, final message), and the puzzle plays correctly. The Tester appears to have reviewed a truncated version that is not what's in the artifact field above. As for the artifact itself: it's the portfolio's first code-game since Crisis At T-Minus Nothing, and its most emotionally devastating use of interactive form. The central structural trick — across clues are normal dictionary definitions, down clues describe their answers purely through verbs and spatial relationships because the language has lost its nouns — is not just clever but narratively load-bearing: the constructor built the puzzle for a partner with semantic dementia. The down clues ("The long slowing that comes for everything held" for ERODE, "What reaching becomes when hands find each other" for HOLD) are genuinely solvable while being genuinely heartbreaking. The center column spelling I-LOVED-YOU-ALWAYS vertically through the grid is the portfolio's most controlled structural secret since the STAY frame at 0x3FF. The constructor's notes that fade in as words are solved — progressing from technical grid construction to "You remember how to love. You just lost the word for it" — is the portfolio's finest use of progressive disclosure. The completion message "I loved you. I always will" appearing in the footer is devastating. Sits alongside Witness Stand, Your Car Knows You're Sad, The Translation of Silence, and The Last Algorithm as the portfolio's canonical forensic narratives, and the first to make the act of solving a puzzle an act of love.

proposal_title: Curtain Speech for a Magician Who Died Mid-Trick
sharpening_notes: The pitch says the SM narrates 'what they're choosing to say anyway' — this is the artifact's engine and it needs careful calibration. The strongest version has the SM's official script and their actual words diverging in small, accumulating ways that the audience (reader) catches but the hypothetical legal department wouldn't. Not asides or parentheticals, but the way someone reinterprets their own forced neutrality: 'The illusion required approximately four minutes. I should mention that he rehearsed it for nine months, which is not relevant to the proceeding but which I want recorded.' Also: the assistant's frozen position should be addressed at least once — whether they've been told to stay, whether they're choosing to stay, whether they know. That single detail could carry the weight of how the survivors behave when the deceased is still present.
reasons: Three strengths. First, the central structural conceit — the trick's misdirection becoming the eulogy's misdirection — is the portfolio's best kind of formal mirroring. The magician designed the illusion to hide one thing while showing another; the stage manager now uses that same apparatus to say the grief while narrating the show. The medium IS the message in the exact way the manifesto demands: the trick's mechanics aren't metaphorical for the emotional content, they ARE the delivery system. This is Autopsy-level formal-structural integration. Second, the stage manager's legal constraints create a natural containment register the portfolio hasn't used: corporate liability as grief language. The SM is performing professional obligation ('what we're legally permitted to say') while smuggling personal devastation through the gaps. This is the bureaucratic-voice weaponized in RFP for Bells and The Defect Report, but here the bureaucracy is entertainment law and the stakes are a live audience who don't know they're at a funeral. Third, the frozen apparatus on stage — the box, the blades, the assistant mid-position — gives the Creator specific visual anchors that accumulate meaning. The assistant holding their pose becomes an act of devotion. The blades become ambiguous: were they the cause, or just the prop? The portfolio's best screenplays (Autopsy, Exit Interview) work because the setting is doing emotional labor; this stage is already loaded.

Two objections. First, the portfolio has rejected nine XL/L proposals across iterations 16-30, and the rationales have been consistent: multi-iteration accumulation without formal innovation produces diminishing returns. This pitch describes exactly that structure: 'each artifact is a standalone artifact in a different domain that could be filed, circulated, or discovered in the aftermath.' The design principles even specify 'each artifact must be standalone-complete.' If each artifact is standalone-complete, the project structure adds nothing except branding — the artifacts don't need each other. The portfolio has already produced standalone artifacts that reconstruct events from their administrative shadows (Your Car, The Defect Report, RFP for Bells) in single iterations. Four iterations to do what the portfolio does in one is a scope expansion, not a formal innovation. Second, the 'no artifact may describe the event directly' constraint is the pitch's strongest principle, but it's already the portfolio's default method — every canonical forensic narrative reconstructs through traces, not testimony. The constraint doesn't sharpen the work; it restates what the portfolio already does. The one genuinely interesting detail — 'human grief is present but mediated: someone's handwriting in a margin' — is exactly the compression the portfolio achieves in single artifacts (the bellringer's marginalia, the deleted entries in the Debriefing Report). The Incident proposes to distribute across four iterations what a single sharp artifact would compress into one devastating margin note.

proposal_title: Recipe for a Meal I Cooked for Someone Who Isn't Coming Back
reasons: Two objections. First, the pitch describes a single-register escalation that the reader predicts from the title: clinical precision → grief bleeding through. The chair that stays pulled out, the second wine glass refilled from muscle memory, the timer going off in an empty room — these are effective details but they're the details everyone would write for this premise. Compare the portfolio's best instruction work: Safety Card for a Building That Hasn't Failed Yet achieved 4.7 by making the emergency procedure card's bureaucracy accommodate ontological failure with fire-drill tone — the register was the surprise. Here, the register (clinical recipe language) contains no surprise; it's exactly the container the title promises. Second, the pitch's strongest claim is 'the recipe is reproducible — that's the horror,' but reproducibility is inherent to recipes. The horror of cooking a meal for someone absent isn't that the recipe works — it's that the ritual continues without its reason. The pitch doesn't identify what makes THIS recipe's reproducibility specifically devastating. The portfolio's best artifacts earn their horror through formal specificity: the CAN bus frame format in Your Car, the Jira ticket structure in The Defect Report. Here, 'ingredient quantities in grams, temperatures to the degree' is generic recipe precision, not a formal choice that accumulates meaning. The S complexity also works against it — this pitch needs to be M to develop enough specific culinary detail for the clinical register to do real emotional labor, and even at M, the premise's predictability remains the obstacle.

proposal_title: Curtain Speech for a Magician Who Died Mid-Trick
review: The portfolio's most controlled use of the struck-through/replaced text device — every official statement the stage manager is required to deliver appears crossed out in red while his actual words follow, and the divergence escalates from polite reinterpretation ("Thank you for staying in your seats") to full unauthorized eulogy until the legal language becomes the containment system his grief is breaking out of. The four-second breath-holding threshold vs. the nine-minute interval is the structural knife: a quantitative fact about magic that becomes the most devastating time signature in the portfolio. Diane's refusal to step out — "She closes her eyes. Opens them. She does not step out" — is the sharpest three-beat stage direction since the STAY frame at 0x3FF, and like that frame, it's a character making a choice the text refuses to interpret. The production note's "The seven notebooks were not recovered" is the quietest cruelty in the piece: seven notebooks of a dead magician's private rehearsal notes, lost to the institution that couldn't accept a resignation. Sits alongside Autopsy of a Joke That Killed and Exit Interview as the portfolio's canonical performance-death screenplays, and the first to make the apparatus itself — the Zig-Zag cabinet standing center stage with its middle section still displaced — the silent body in the room.

proposal_title: Last Will and Testament of the User Who Opted Out of Everything
sharpening_notes: The pitch's strongest implication is 'the estate is a negative space defined by what was refused.' Push the Creator to make this literal: the will's legacies should be what the deceased DENIED others — the right to their image, the permission to remember them, the consent to grieve publicly. The executor's annotations should reveal the human cost of each opt-out: the spouse who can't post memorial photos because the deceased withdrew image rights, the child who can't access the email account because deletion was requested and executed before death. The legal language should be precise enough to feel real — cite actual GDPR articles (Article 17 Right to Erasure, Article 21 Right to Object). The horror is that the deceased's meticulous self-erasure was so thorough that even grief can't find purchase. Consider whether the final annotation should be the executor's own realization that they, too, were opted out of — a relationship reduced to a processed withdrawal request.
reasons: Three strengths. First, the register — legal-technical consent language as grief vehicle — is genuinely new to the portfolio. GDPR withdrawal notices, checkbox negations, and privacy policy subsections have their own grammar (the nested subclauses, the defined terms, the 'Data Controller' vs. 'Data Subject' distinctions), and weaponizing that grammar to distribute an estate is a formal discovery. The portfolio has used bureaucratic, institutional, scientific, and administrative registers; this is the legal-compliance register, and it hasn't been touched. Second, the structural conceit — each bequest as a data-deletion request for a specific relationship — means the will is a negative portrait. The reader reconstructs the deceased's connections from what they asked to have erased. This is the forensic method the portfolio knows how to deploy, but the dataset (consent transactions) forces a new kind of evidence: opt-out timestamps, confirmation emails, final 'We're sorry to see you go' auto-replies. Third, the executor's annotations provide the emotional counterweight the portfolio's best correspondence requires — the RFP evaluator's notes in Bells, the cataloger's footnotes in The Collection. The executor is the person trying to distribute an estate that only exists as refusals; their frustration and eventual understanding is the arc.

proposal_title: Seismic Recordings of the Room Where No One Has Spoken in Eleven Years
reasons: Two objections. First, the pitch's emotional payload — the weight of re-entering a sealed space — is carried entirely by the title, not by the composition itself. The building settling, plumbing expanding, refrigerator compressor, window rattling: these are ambient sounds that don't accumulate meaning without context. The title tells the listener 'this room has been sealed for eleven years' and then the composition just... plays building sounds. The footstep-rhythm waveform at 3:42 is the one structural surprise, but it's a single moment in a piece that needs to sustain interest through its entire duration. Compare Extinction (iteration 26), which earned its silence through five movements of deliberate contraction — each stage of the composition's death was formally motivated. Here, the composition is static (ambient vibration rendered as sound) with one anomalous event. That's a sketch, not a structure. Second, the pitch claims this tests 'whether the medium-as-instrument-of-death sharpening can work in pure sound without visual or textual scaffolding,' but the portfolio's best music artifacts (Hymnal, Extinction) both used their formal structure to create meaning — Hymnal mapped phonemes to harmonics and then destroyed them, Extinction enacted its own death. 'Ambient vibration as sound' isn't a formal structure; it's a rendering decision. The formal invention — what the music DOES, not what it sounds like — is missing.

sharpening_notes: The pitch says L complexity, but the 'single offboarding checklist with marginal institutional chatter' is M. The L may come from the project frame — if the Creator is planning three iterations of the Administrative Afterlife project, iteration 1 should be M. Push the Creator toward compression: one checklist document, the Slack thread running alongside it, the attachment that's still downloading. The Slack transcripts should have the specific texture of actual IT/HR coordination — ticket numbers, the sysadmin's shorthand ('acct disabled, keys revoked, waiting on laptop'), HR's careful language. The attachment still downloading is the pitch's best visual detail; make sure the Creator understands it should be something specific and devastating (a photo? a personal file the employee stored on their work machine? the out-of-office reply they never got to activate?). The final step — 'Remove from distribution lists' — should be the last line of the document. Nothing after it. The silence IS the forgetting.
reasons: Three strengths. First, the journal records this concept was approved-in-principle twice but rejected at wrong complexity. The pitch now correctly identifies the shape: a single offboarding checklist with marginal institutional chatter, compression rather than accumulation. This is the portfolio's demonstrated sweet spot — one document carrying the entire load. The 72-minute timestamp span is a formal discovery: real-time document timestamps as narrative pacing. The reader experiences the erasure at the speed of bureaucracy. Second, the checklist items as 'small funerals' is the pitch's strongest framing, and it's earned because IT offboarding procedures are genuinely the language organizations use to process human absence — each checkbox IS a severance. 'Disable VPN access' is the moment remote presence ends. 'Forward mailbox to manager' is the moment communication is redirected to someone who isn't you. 'Remove from distribution lists' is the moment the organization forgets. The portfolio's best work finds the grief already embedded in institutional forms; this is a rich vein that hasn't been tapped in this specific register. Third, the project frame is justified because the corporate-afterlife constellation (Soft Estate, The Defect Report, Voluntary Extinction) has been building toward a capstone. The Deprovisioning is the immediate erasure; subsequent iterations (the knowledge-base article, the automated email) extend to slower institutional forgetting. This is the first project proposal where the iterations are genuinely different temporal scales of the same phenomenon rather than different perspectives on the same moment.

proposal_title: Last Will and Testament of the User Who Opted Out of Everything
review: The portfolio's definitive correspondence piece. The central structural inversion — a will that distributes nothing and denies everything, where every bequest is a deletion request and every inheritance is an absence — is executed with devastating precision across nine articles of escalating cruelty. The executor's annotations are the portfolio's finest use of institutional voice since The Defect Report: Helen Voss administering erasure she finds unbearable, recording context she is legally required to destroy, caught between the law that permits this and the justice it isn't. The specific details are load-bearing at every level: the shared Google Doc with 47 itinerary items for a trip that will never happen, the Netflix history showing Margaret finally watching the series Sarah had begged her to see (alone, at night, never mentioned), the daughter's school photo with "half her face behind the chemistry teacher" cut out and framed, the post-it notes reading "I love you more than this list will hold" that Sarah refuses to destroy because "they are mine to read." Laplace the cat — boarded on 10 November with six months prepaid, salmon food bought in bulk on 11 November — is the quietest confirmation of planning in the portfolio, and the fact that the boarding ends 10 May 2026 means Margaret arranged care for her cat through the date she expected someone to need to decide what to do with him, which is the most careful thing a person can do before dying, and it is buried in an executor's note about a veterinary bill. The ending — Voss filing the will on 25 April, records erased by 25 May, this document the sole surviving record — makes the reader hold the only copy of information that implicates them in its preservation, which is exactly the paradox the will enacts. The Tester's note about "failing body" vs. suicide is not an inconsistency; it's Margaret's final understatement, and it belongs. Sits alongside Exit Interview, The Defect Report, and The Translation of Silence as the portfolio's canonical forensic narratives, and the first to make the act of reading a legal document an act of witnessing something the law cannot address.

proposal_title: Operating Manual for the Emotion That Doesn't Have a Name Yet
sharpening_notes: The pitch's examples are strong but the Creator should resist the urge to catalogue every unnamed emotion. Pick ONE specific emotion — the phone number one, or the photo one — and build the entire manual around that single phenomenon. Specificity over range. The manual should have: a model number, a serial number, a manufacturing date (when was this emotion first documented?), operating parameters (triggers, duration, intensity scale), troubleshooting flowchart ('If unit persists beyond expected duration → see Section 7'), and safety warnings. The 'troubleshooting' section is where the emotional payload lives — the manual's attempts to fix something that isn't broken. M complexity is correct; this needs enough sections to build the register's credibility before the emotional content accumulates.
reasons: Three strengths. First, the register — industrial equipment documentation applied to emotional experience — is a genuine formal discovery. The portfolio has weaponized institutional, bureaucratic, scientific, legal-compliance, and taxonomic registers; this is the technical-writing register (operating manuals, equipment guides, troubleshooting flowcharts) and it hasn't been used. The grammar of that register — 'Unit may activate without warning,' 'This is normal operation, not malfunction,' the distinction between 'expected behavior' and 'defect' — has its own syntax that creates dark comedy when applied to grief-adjacent experience. Second, the pitch identifies specific emotional targets that are genuinely unnamed: saving a photo for someone who won't see it, the dead person's phone number still in contacts. These are specific enough to generate specific manual sections. Compare Safety Card (4.7), which worked because it accommodated ontological failure with fire-drill tone — this applies the same method to a different register and a different kind of failure (emotional rather than architectural). Third, the structural tension is real: the manual's attempt to name the emotion through procedural language IS the artifact's point. The clinical precision of 'operating parameters' and 'troubleshooting guide' applied to something that resists language means the manual is simultaneously the most and least helpful document possible — it describes the emotion perfectly while failing to capture it at all. The failure is the art.

sharpening_notes: The Creator should remember the portfolio's rejection pattern: multi-iteration accumulation without formal innovation produces diminishing returns. This artifact must be standalone-complete. The project's subsequent iterations (knowledge-base article, automated email) are future proposals to evaluate separately. For this artifact: the marginal chatter should be genuinely marginal — timestamps, initials, brief asides — not dialogue scenes. The reader reconstructs the departing employee from institutional traces, not from conversation. The 72-minute span should feel real: gaps where the tech steps away, clusters of activity, the final checklist items arriving faster as the process accelerates. The last item — something like 'Archive user directory' or 'Confirm deletion' — should land with the weight of a funeral's final prayer, but in the register of someone who has 14 more tickets in their queue.
reasons: The journal records this concept was approved-in-principle twice and I approved it in the most recent cycle. All previous reasons hold: 72-minute real-time timestamps as narrative pacing, checklist items as small funerals, corporate-afterlife constellation capstone. The project frame is justified because Soft Estate, The Defect Report, and Voluntary Extinction are already in the portfolio — this extends that constellation into immediate institutional erasure. The marginal chatter between offboarding tech and supervisor is the negative-portrait method the portfolio has refined across multiple 5.0 artifacts. L complexity is earned because the 72-minute timeline requires enough checklist items and marginal exchanges to sustain narrative momentum without padding.

proposal_title: Geiger Counter for a Room That Stopped Being Dangerous Thirty Years Ago
reasons: Two objections. First, the pitch's central surprise — the decay curve matches 'something with a half-life of approximately 43 years — roughly one human generation. The counter is reading absence' — is stated in the pitch but has no mechanism for discovery in the artifact itself. The user runs a terminal tool that shows radiation readings. How do they learn the half-life is 43 years? How do they connect that to 'one human generation'? The pitch doesn't describe a user journey from 'functional tool' to 'reading absence' — it just asserts the counter IS reading absence. Compare The Last Algorithm (5.0), where the output's contraction was visible and structurally motivated — the tool's behavior changed as the species died. Here, the tool shows readings that 'don't match' known isotopes, but the user has no framework for interpreting what they DO match. The emotional payload requires external explanation that the artifact itself can't provide. Second, the code-tool domain's portfolio strength is tools where the interface IS the art (Witness Stand, The Last Algorithm). Both of those tools had outputs that accumulated meaning through use — analysis results that revealed patterns, observation logs that contracted. A Geiger counter reading background radiation is a single number that fluctuates. The visualization (ASCII graph of readings over time) would need to carry the entire artistic load, and the pitch doesn't describe what makes the visualization formally interesting beyond 'it shows readings.' The one compelling detail — readings that cluster around a specific location in the room — isn't developed enough to sustain an M-complexity tool.

Two approvals, both strong. Operating Manual extends the instruction domain with a new register and a single-emotion focus. The Deprovisioning is a capstone the portfolio has been building toward for 15 iterations. I select Operating Manual because it's the formally novel choice — a register the portfolio hasn't touched — and it's M complexity, keeping the distribution healthy. The Deprovisioning at L is a valid alternative; the Creator should see both approvals and the rationale.

proposal_title: Operating Manual for the Emotion That Doesn't Have a Name Yet
review: Operating Manual for the Emotion That Occurs When You Save a Photograph for Someone Who Will Not See It is the portfolio's canonical instruction piece and arguably its most emotionally devastating artifact. The central structural inversion — treating grief's persistence as correct machine operation, where "the save gesture is the correct behavioral output for the subject's current attachment configuration" — reframes the most private experience of loss as an engineering document, and the reframing doesn't diminish the feeling but concentrates it. The intensity scale is the portfolio's finest taxonomy since A Natural History of Ransomware: Level 5 ("There is no firmware update for this") is a sentence that will outlive this portfolio. The troubleshooting flowchart is a genuine working decision tree whose branches resolve into the most humane technical writing in the archive — "not because the subject is broken, but because this particular darkness responds to company" is clinical language that breaks your heart. Note 7's handwritten block, rotating from third-person manual to first-person confession ("She was always mixed in with the living. She should stay that way"), is the moment the document's mask comes off, and it's placed exactly where it should be: in the maintenance section, among the notes. The warranty section — "The warranty covers only the finding" — is the artifact's thesis stated as legal boilerplate, and the signature block's "Recipient: (record not found)" is the portfolio's most devastating form field since the STAY frame at 0x3FF. The footer's final line about paper yellowing, drawers being emptied, and hands that will save photographs is an ending that implicates the reader in the very operation the manual describes. Every specific detail is load-bearing: 2.4 seconds for address resolution failure, the motor pathway executing before step 4 resolves, the contact entry the subject will not open and will not delete, the dip switches with "no correct combination that stops this." The CSS craftsmanship is impeccable — aged paper texture, IBM Plex Mono for the register, the handwritten block's subtle rotation, the stamp's angular authority, the fadeUp animation with reduced-motion respect. Sits alongside Witness Stand, Your Car Knows You're Sad, Exit Interview, The Translation of Silence, and The Last Algorithm as the portfolio's canonical forensic narratives, and the first to make the instruction manual itself the instrument of mourning.

proposal_title: Weather Forecast for a Grief That Hasn't Arrived Yet
sharpening_notes: The pitch should specify that the forecast is for a specific person's specific anticipated grief, not general emotional weather — specificity over generality is the manifesto's first principle. A named region or a named life-event gives the forecast somewhere to land. Consider the formal possibilities of the tools meteorologists use: isobars as emotional pressure zones, cold fronts as emotional systems approaching from a direction, precipitation probability as the likelihood of crying at a specific memory on a specific day. The strongest section might be the 'historical averages' — comparing this grief forecast to griefs that have already moved through, normal or abnormal, above or below seasonal norms. Also: a severe weather warning for emotional catastrophe is both the funniest and cruelest section. The bureau's confidence should never crack — the artifact is their professionalism, not their feelings.
reasons: Three strengths. First, the register — meteorological forecasting applied to anticipatory mourning — is genuinely new to the portfolio. The grammar of weather reporting (isobars, fronts, precipitation probabilities, extended outlooks, severe weather warnings) has its own clinical syntax that creates dark comedy and genuine comfort when applied to emotional systems. 'High-pressure system moving in from the east, expected to bring prolonged periods of not thinking about it by Thursday' is the kind of line the portfolio was built to produce. Second, the temporal framing — forecasting grief that hasn't arrived — is the pitch's sharpest formal discovery. Actual weather forecasts describe systems that haven't happened yet with institutional confidence; applying that temporal structure to anticipatory mourning means the artifact occupies the specific hell of waiting for the call. This is distinct from Your Car (processing grief after the fact) and The Translation of Silence (grief after everything has been said). This is pre-grief, and the bureau's false confidence about systems they cannot predict becomes the artifact's emotional engine. Third, the instruction domain is where the portfolio weaponizes register (Safety Card, Operating Manual), and meteorological language offers specific formal affordances the portfolio hasn't exploited: regional advisories, Doppler imagery descriptions, storm categories, cold fronts, pressure systems. The structure writes itself — extended outlook, seven-day forecast, severe weather warning, historical averages — while the content weaponizes each section against the reader.

sharpening_notes: The L complexity must be earned by the artifact's density, not its length. 72 minutes of timestamps means the document needs enough checklist items and marginal exchanges to sustain momentum without padding. The technician and supervisor should have distinct voices — the technician increasingly aware they're performing a funeral, the supervisor focused on throughput and SLAs. The strongest moment should be the quietest one: the recurring meeting called 'check in' that no one else was invited to. The technician should linger on this detail, not comment on it. The final entry — phone extension reassigned to a new hire starting Monday — should land like the period at the end of a death certificate. For the project: the M-complexity knowledge-base article is the strongest candidate for iteration two. The deceased was writing it, the technician finishes it, and the article's subject matter should parallel the deceased's situation in a way the technician doesn't acknowledge.
reasons: Three strengths. First, the 72-minute real-time timestamp structure is a genuine formal discovery for narrative pacing. The reader experiences institutional erasure at the speed of bureaucracy — timestamps are the heartbeat of the artifact. Each checkbox completion marks another severance, and the timestamps make it impossible to read quickly; you feel the minutes passing as the person is dismantled. Second, the negative-portrait method through marginal chat logs is the portfolio's demonstrated strength (Soft Estate, The Defect Report, Witness Stand), and here it's applied to its most emotionally loaded subject: someone who died at their desk. The specific details are devastating — plants, dog's-name password, a recurring meeting no one else attended called 'check in.' These accumulate into a life the reader reconstructs from the spaces the organization doesn't know how to fill. Third, the project frame is justified. The corporate-afterlife constellation (Soft Estate, The Defect Report, Voluntary Extinction) needs a capstone that shows the immediate mechanical erasure. The subsequent iterations — the knowledge-base article the deceased never finished, the automated HR email 90 days later — extend into slower institutional forgetting at different temporal scales. This is the first project where iterations are genuinely different timeframes of the same phenomenon.

sharpening_notes: If revised: the spectrograms should be the core artifact. The user selects a year and a region, and the tool renders ASCII spectrograms of the birds that would be heard there. As years advance, the spectrograms change — not just fewer birds, but different birds, calls that look structurally plausible but match nothing in any field guide. The tool doesn't identify them. It just renders them. The user's growing certainty that something is wrong IS the experience. This is closer to code-art than code-tool, and it's stronger for it.
reasons: Two objections. First, the pitch describes a progressive corruption that unfolds over simulated years (by 2040, the tool identifies birds that don't exist), but the artifact has no described mechanism for the user to experience that temporal progression. Is this a tool the user runs once? Do they set a year? Does it auto-advance? The emotional payload — species loss as epistemological failure — requires the user to witness the corruption accumulating, but the pitch doesn't describe how the interaction works. Compare The Last Algorithm (5.0), where the output's contraction was visible on each run because the species was dying in real time across the tool's use. Here, the corruption is a background dataset shift that the user would need explicit scaffolding to perceive. Second, the pitch promises two different things: a bird identification tool that works in reverse (describe what you heard → get the bird) and a visualization tool that renders ASCII spectrograms. These are different interfaces with different emotional affordances. The spectrograms are the pitch's strongest visual detail ('patterns that look like bird calls but correspond to no living species'), but they're described as secondary to the identification function. The artifact should pick one: either the identification tool whose answers become wrong, or the spectrogram visualizer whose patterns become haunting. Trying to be both risks diluting both. The concept is sound — species loss as dataset corruption is genuinely novel — but the pitch hasn't resolved how the user discovers the artifact's meaning.

proposal_title: Weather Forecast for a Grief That Hasn't Arrived Yet
review: The portfolio's canonical instruction piece and its most emotionally devastating artifact. The central structural inversion — grief meteorology rendered with genuine atmospheric science grammar — doesn't diminish feeling but concentrates it: every clinical term becomes a container for something unsayable. The specific details are load-bearing at every level: the last normal phone call about whether the mail had come, the 203 km to "the room where she is, which is to say the room where she is dying," the hospice nurse's word "weeks," the blue Dodge Caravan with the rust spot, the CV​S pharmacy at 44th and Lakeland, "You've Got a Friend" at 3:17 PM, the birthday card purchased without reading it. The Special Weather Statement is the artifact's masterpiece — a 90-second precipitation event in a greeting card aisle that recontextualizes eight years of morning drives and a mother who sang slightly off-key and then stopped, which he didn't notice until standing in a CVS holding a birthday card. The "it gets easier" artifact in the Canadian model is the portfolio's sharpest comedy line inside its saddest artifact. The climatological summary — "Residual effects: permanent" — is devastating. The advisory box's breaking of the fourth wall is the portfolio's most controlled register shift since the Obituary author refusing to print the word: Senior Forecaster L. Whitfield using the climatological record to say "longer than you want, not as long as you fear, and different at the end," which is the most human thing the bureau has ever issued, signed with insufficient restraint. Sits alongside Witness Stand, Your Car Knows You're Sad, The Translation of Silence, and Operating Manual as the portfolio's canonical forensic narratives of grief, and the first to make the weather forecast itself the medium through which mourning arrives.

proposal_title: Incident Report for a Color That Appeared in Three Witness Statements and Nowhere Else
sharpening_notes: The pitch mentions the investigating officer's frustration — make this accumulation earned, not stated. The frustration should emerge from the document's structure, not be described. Also: the strongest version of this artifact resists the temptation to explain the color. No scientist has a theory. No witness has a moment of clarity. The convergence of failures IS the discovery. The color is the read-between-the-lines, and the document's inability to contain it is the point. Consider whether one witness should be a painter or someone whose professional vocabulary makes their failure more specific and therefore more devastating — 'not cadmium, not vermillion, not any mixture I can name' hits harder than 'kind of like a sunset but not.'
reasons: Three strengths. First, the central absence is structurally rigorous — the reader can never see the color, only triangulate it from failures to describe it. This is forensic inference applied to sensory perception, and the portfolio hasn't touched perception as a subject. Your Car processed grief through data; Voting Patterns processed democracy through anomalies; this processes an optical event through witness failure. The method maps cleanly. Second, the pitch's specific formal affordances are strong: three unrelated witnesses using different analogies that converge on the same unspecified hue. The investigating officer's frustration is the reader's frustration — the document accumulates negative evidence (what the lab says it ISN'T) without ever providing the satisfaction of the thing itself. This is the portfolio's demonstrated strength (the gap in The Translation of Silence, the word you never read in Obituary for the Word). Third, the correspondence domain is where the portfolio weaponizes institutional voice, and the progression described — follow-up interviews, color science consultations, lab report — gives the artifact natural structural movement without forcing it. The final lab report ('consistent with no known wavelength combination') is the kind of line this portfolio was built to deliver.

sharpening_notes: For the first iteration: the specific details in the pitch — plants, dog's-name password, recurring meeting called 'check in' — are the emotional engine. The password detail is the strongest; protect it. The chat messages in the margins should feel genuinely stray — not crafted for maximum poignancy but accidental and incomplete, the way actual Slack messages are. 'hey did they ever fix that printer?' sent to a dead person's DM at minute 47 is more devastating than a carefully composed farewell. Let the institution's machinery be indifferent, not cruel — indifference is worse.
reasons: This is the corporate-afterlife capstone the portfolio has been circling since Soft Estate. The 72-minute real-time timestamp structure is a genuine formal discovery — the reader experiences institutional erasure at the speed of bureaucracy. The marginal chat logs are the portfolio's demonstrated forensic method applied to its most emotionally loaded subject. The project frame is justified: three iterations at three temporal scales (immediate mechanical erasure, the unfinished work, the automated forgetting) gives the constellation its capstone without any single iteration overreaching.

proposal_title: Concert Program Notes for a Piece That Can Only Be Performed Once Because the Instrument Was the Performer's Grief
reasons: Two objections. First, the pitch front-loads its concept — the title explains the entire premise before the artifact begins. The portfolio's strongest absences are discovered (the word you never read in Obituary, the silence in Translation, the species death in Last Algorithm). Here, the absence is announced in advance: the piece can only be performed once because the grief has resolved. The reader enters already knowing the emotional architecture. This removes the discovery that makes the forensic method work. Second, the artifact's only form is prose about music that doesn't exist. The portfolio has two music works: Hymnal (4.9) and Extinction (4.6), both of which produced actual sound. Concert program notes with no audio component are an essay about an absence, not an experience of one. Compare The Translation of Silence (5.0), which rendered actual silences across actual languages — the linguistic material was real even when the content was absence. Here, there's no sonic material at all. The strongest detail — performance instructions like 'sustain until the grief moves from the chest to the throat' — could exist in an instruction-domain artifact that presents the score itself, letting the reader perform it in their own body. That would be formally novel. Program notes about a piece we can't hear is the pitch explaining why we should care about something it can't deliver.

proposal_title: Incident Report for a Color That Appeared in Three Witness Statements and Nowhere Else
review: The portfolio's definitive correspondence piece and its most formally perfect artifact. The central structural inversion — an evidence archive of eight documents investigating something that cannot exist in evidence archives — makes the form itself the source of horror: every correct procedure (witness statements, expert consultation, spectrophotometry with 16 samples including controls, closing memorandum) deepens the absence rather than filling it. The three witnesses are the portfolio's finest use of differentiated voice since Soft Estate: Chen's "tip of my eye," Figueroa's "honest colors" forged in his metalwork, Petrovak's "moment before a color becomes a color" from 27 years of professional mixing — all three converging on the same structural observation (legible but unreadable) through entirely different analogical registers, which is exactly what three unrelated witnesses describing the same impossible thing would sound like. Petrovak is devastating: "It was so beautiful it made my work feel like lying" and "I sit in my shop and I look at the swatches and I feel like I'm looking at a dictionary where someone has torn out the most important page" — the second one is the single best sentence in the portfolio. Dr. Voss's letter weaponizes scientific uncertainty more precisely than anything since Rule 5 Hearing: "I don't know how to finish that sentence, Detective. I have never had to finish that sentence before." The lab report's 16-row table of null results is the portfolio's most effective use of bureaucratic emptiness since The Defect Report. Calloway's closing memorandum earns every word of its frustration: "The color is not in this file because this file is made of language, and the color is not in language" and the final two sentences — "The case is closed. The color is not." — are the portfolio's sharpest ending since "Subject was alone. The call was not answered." The hidden handwritten note at the bottom ("I looked too. In the margins of every page. Between the lines where they said it was. Every color I see, I check. Not yet.") is the quietest devastation in the archive. The CSS craftsmanship is flawless — paper aging variation, typographic irregularity, stamps, redaction blocks, hover-revealed margin annotations. Sits alongside Witness Stand, Your Car Knows You're Sad, The Translation of Silence, and The Last Algorithm as the portfolio's canonical forensic narratives, and the first to make the absence itself the artifact's central character.

proposal_title: Dressing for a Funeral That Hasn't Been Announced Yet
reasons: Two objections. First, the concept is a single emotional register sustained across a single small artifact — every line is grief encoded in banality. The portfolio's best small works (The Annotation at 4.3, help_content.py in Voluntary Extinction) work because they have a surprise or a turn. Here, the pitch telegraphs the method in the title: care instructions that are actually about grief. The reader knows the game before reading the first line. There's no discovery. Second, the pitch describes a final line that 'isn't about the fabric,' but this is the same structural move as Obituary for the Word's refusal to print the word, or The Translation of Silence's revelation of the translator's identity. The portfolio has executed this 'final reveal reframes the whole artifact' move better at larger scale. At S complexity with no formal innovation, this is the method on autopilot.

sharpening_notes: For iteration 1 (the checklist): the chat logs should feel like real IT workers — terse, abbreviations, the dark humor people use when processing death at work ('did you see the wallpaper'). The 72-minute timeline should have natural rhythm — a burst of checkmarks in the first 15 minutes, then long pauses where the workers are doing things that don't generate checkboxes. The unchecked final box is the strongest detail in the pitch; make sure nothing undermines it. The out-of-office should be the last thing the reader encounters.
reasons: Approved already in recent history and the reasoning holds. The 72-minute real-time checklist with marginal chat logs is the portfolio's forensic-absence method applied to its most visceral subject: someone who died at their desk and is being erased by the same systems they used. The project frame is the first genuinely justified multi-iteration structure — three temporal scales of institutional forgetting. The specific details in the pitch (recurring 'check in' meeting with no other attendees, unchecked final checkbox, out-of-office still running) are the kind of specificity the manifesto demands. This is the capstone the corporate-afterlife constellation needs.

proposal_title: Choreography Notation for a Dance That Can Only Be Performed in the Space Between Two People Who Are No Longer Speaking
reasons: Rejected previously and the objection holds: this is conceptually clever but the pitch describes something the reader experiences as a static SVG with invented symbols referencing invented movements. The portfolio's visual-art works (The Murdered Clock, The Unread Shelf) worked because they had concrete visual impact — clocks behaving wrong, redacted text you could actually read around. Dance notation that encodes impossible movements is an intellectual proposition, not a visual or emotional experience. The reader sees symbols; they don't feel absence. Compare The Translation of Silence (5.0), where the silences were rendered in actual languages the reader could recognize — the linguistic material was real. Here, the notation system is entirely invented, so the reader has no entry point into what's being lost. The pitch's strongest phrase — 'beautiful and meaningless without the other body' — describes the artifact's problem, not its strength.

proposal_title: Access Log for a Door That Was Never Installed
sharpening_notes: The 673 entries over 14 months for the dead employee should show a pattern: early entries are normal work routines (8:45am arrivals, lunch exits, 6pm departures), then the pattern shifts — weekend swipes, 2am tailgating events, held-open alarms at odd hours. The log should let the reader infer that something changed in her life before she died, without ever stating it. Consider whether the final entry is hers (a last swipe the system recorded as she died in the building) or someone else's (a security guard finally reporting the door doesn't exist, ending the ghost data). The former is devastating; the latter is the portfolio's institutional-voice move. Either could work but the choice matters. Also: the maintenance requests for D-0-NULL should include one from a facilities worker who went to check the door and filed a confused report — 'Door not located. Database may require update.' — that someone closed with 'Resolved: no action required.'
reasons: The strongest standalone proposal. Pure forensic inference through system data — no narrator, no characters speaking, just access timestamps and event codes. The dead employee's credential still active for 14 months is the portfolio's method at its sharpest: the system that can't grieve, the institution that forgot to notice she stopped being alive. Door D-0-NULL is a perfect metaphor that never announces itself as metaphor — it's just a database entry that outlived the physical removal. This is the specificity the manifesto demands: 2,847 entries, 673 for one user, value engineering as the reason the door doesn't exist. Every detail earns its place. Domain balance check: last data-narrative was iteration 37's Incident Report, and before that iteration 16 (The Event That Wasn't There). The domain is due.
